questions.q: In a zero-indexed array, if you want to access the element at position 5, what index do you use?
options: It depends on the programming language
questions.explanation: Zero-indexed arrays count from 0, so position 5 is at index 5. Position 1 would be at index 0. The indexing system is standard across all programming languages for arrays.

questions.q: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in a static array by index?
questions.explanation: Array access by index is constant time O(1) because the memory address can be computed directly using the base address plus the index. This is independent of the array size.

questions.q: Which operation is most expensive in a dynamic array when it needs to resize?
options: Copying all elements to a larger memory block
questions.explanation: Resizing requires copying all existing elements to a new memory location, which takes O(n) time. While sorting is also expensive, resizing is specifically what causes the performance hit during dynamic array growth.

questions.q: What is the average time complexity of inserting an element at the beginning of a dynamic array?
questions.explanation: Inserting at the beginning requires shifting all existing elements one position to the right, which takes O(n) time. Insertions at the end are O(1) amortized.

questions.q: In string manipulation, concatenating two strings of lengths m and n using naive concatenation typically has what time complexity?
questions.explanation: Concatenating strings requires copying all characters from both strings into a new location, which takes O(m + n) time. The distractor O(m × n) assumes nested operations that don't occur in concatenation.

questions.q: What distinguishes a dynamic array from a static array?
options: Dynamic arrays can only store integers
Dynamic arrays can grow and shrink at runtime, while static arrays have fixed size
Dynamic arrays are always faster than static arrays
Static arrays cannot be modified after creation
questions.explanation: Dynamic arrays allocate memory dynamically and can resize at runtime (e.g., ArrayList in Java, vector in C++), while static arrays have fixed size determined at compile time. Both can store any data type and can be modified.

questions.q: A string is stored as an array of characters. If a string has 10 characters, how much space does a null terminator add to its memory footprint?
questions.explanation: A null terminator is a single null character (\0) added at the end of C-style strings to mark the end. It adds exactly 1 byte. This is separate from character encoding variations.

questions.q: When performing a binary search on a sorted array, what is the time complexity?
questions.explanation: Binary search eliminates half the remaining elements with each comparison, achieving O(log n) time complexity. This is much faster than linear search O(n) and works only on sorted arrays.

questions.q: What is the worst-case time complexity of finding a substring within a string using a naive approach?
questions.explanation: Naive substring search checks each position in the main string (n positions) and for each position compares up to m characters of the substring, resulting in O(m × n) worst case. Algorithms like KMP reduce this to O(m + n).

questions.q: In an array of size n, how many comparisons are needed in the best case to find an element using linear search?
questions.explanation: In the best case, the element is found immediately at the first position, requiring only 1 comparison. However, the worst case requires n comparisons and average case requires n/2 comparisons.
